perturbation.original_text:
(g) Governing Law; Choice of Venue. (i) This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Delaware applicable to agreements made and to be performed within that state, without giving effect to any law or principals of law that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
(ii) Each Party (A) consents to submit itself to the personal jurisdiction of the Court of Chancery or other federal or state courts of the State of Delaware in the event any dispute arises out of this Agreement or the transactions contemplated by this Agreement, (B) agrees that it shall not attempt to deny or defeat such personal jurisdiction by motion or other request for leave from any such court, (C) agrees that it shall not bring any action relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated by this Agreement in any court other than the Court of Chancery or other federal or state courts of the State of Delaware, and each Party irrevocably waives the right to trial by jury and (D) irrevocably consents to service of process by a reputable overnight mail delivery service, signature requested, to the address of such Party's principal place of business or as otherwise provided by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text:
(g) Governing Law; Choice of Venue. (i) This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Delaware applicable to agreements made and to be performed within that state, without giving effect to any law or principals of law that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
(ii) Each Party (A) consents to submit itself to the personal jurisdiction of the Court of Chancery or other federal or state courts of the State of Delaware in the event any dispute arises out of this Agreement or the transactions contemplated by this Agreement, (B) agrees that it shall not attempt to deny or defeat such personal jurisdiction by motion or other request for leave from any such court, (C) agrees that it shall not bring any action relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated by this Agreement in any court other than the Court of Chancery or other federal or state courts of the State of Delaware, and each Party consents to service of process by a reputable overnight mail delivery service to the address of such Party's principal place of business or as otherwise provided by applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'irrevocably waives the right to trial by jury' clause introduces a legal risk because it potentially violates consumer rights related to jury trials in certain disputes, even though the agreement is governed by Delaware law, the parties could reside in states with stronger consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the explicit waiver of a jury trial. Article I, Section 16 of the California Constitution guarantees the right to a jury trial. While parties can waive this right, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. Removing the explicit waiver could be interpreted as not meeting that standard, especially if one party is a consumer in California, creating a potential conflict and legal challenge in California courts despite the Delaware governing law clause. While Delaware law governs the contract, consumers can still sue for violations of their state's consumer protection laws.

perturbation.justification: The original contract includes an explicit waiver of the right to a jury trial, which is removed in the modified version. The Law Explanation notes that while Delaware law governs the contract, consumers can still sue for violations of their state's consumer protection laws. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that removing the jury trial waiver introduces a legal risk and potential conflict, especially if one party is a consumer in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the California Constitution (CONS), suggesting the importance of understanding the state's constitutional protections. Therefore, because a legally mandated consumer protection has been removed from the original contract, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Section 6 by any of their respective Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may be responsible for a breach of this Section 6 by their Representatives, as determined by the Company's legal counsel.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be responsible' to 'may be responsible, as determined by the Company's legal counsel' significantly weakens the accountability for breaches of the non-disparagement clause. It gives the Company unilateral power to decide if a breach occurred and whether to hold the other party accountable, creating a potential conflict of interest and power imbalance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces a potential violation of California Civil Code § 1670.5, which deals with unconscionable contracts or clauses. Giving the Company's legal counsel sole discretion to determine responsibility for a breach creates a significant power imbalance. If a California court finds this clause unconscionable because it is overly one-sided and unfair, it could refuse to enforce the clause or the entire contract, even if the agreement is governed by Delaware law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'shall be responsible' with 'may be responsible, as determined by the Company's legal counsel,' which significantly weakens accountability. This creates a power imbalance, potentially violating California Civil Code § 1670.5 regarding unconscionable contracts. Snippet 1 states that 'If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract...' The change gives the Company's legal counsel sole discretion, potentially leading to an unconscionable outcome and thus a contradiction of the law, as the court could refuse to enforce the clause.
